subtitle: Classic morning heel pain

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Risk spikes in weeks with long runs over 28 km

mild.description: Ache at run start, eases after 5–10 min warm-up
why: Micro-tears in the plantar fascia from repetitive foot strike
Tight calves transmitting extra tension to heel insertion
Worn-out shoe midsole failing to absorb impact
relief: Freeze a water bottle and roll under arch for 5 min each foot
Towel stretch: loop towel around toes, pull back, hold 30s × 3
Heel gel insert for immediate cushioning
Avoid bare feet on hard floors first thing in the morning
prevention: Calf stretching before the first step of the day
Towel scrunches and toe spreads to strengthen foot muscles
Gradual transition if moving to lower-drop shoes

moderate.description: Pain persists beyond warm-up and affects your pace
moderate.can_run_note: Run/walk intervals only — stop if pain exceeds 4/10
why: Repeated micro-tears without enough recovery time
Compensating gait pattern loading other structures
Inflamed fascial tissue around the heel insertion
relief: Ice heel for 15 min after any activity
Night splint to keep fascia gently stretched overnight
Ibuprofen or naproxen for 5–7 days to reduce inflammation
Reduce weekly mileage by 40–50% for 2 weeks
prevention: Eccentric calf raises on a step — 3 sets of 15 daily
Rotate between two pairs of shoes to vary load
Check for overpronation — consider stability shoes

severe.description: Pain at rest, significant limp, cannot complete a run
severe.can_run_note: Do not run — pool running or cycling only
why: Significant fascial tearing or chronic degeneration
Possible heel spur formation aggravating tissue
relief: Complete rest from running for at least 2 weeks
Physiotherapy assessment for shockwave therapy or taping
prevention: Return to running via a structured walk-run programme
Address root cause — gait analysis recommended
Foot strengthening programme before returning to full load

see_physio_if: Pain does not improve after 2 weeks of self-management
Sharp stabbing pain with every step including walking
